findings.title: Module-level mutable global ITERATION + global rebind in main() is brittle
findings.evidence: ITERATION is defined at module level as WORKSPACE / 'iteration-1', then `main()` does `global ITERATION; ITERATION = WORKSPACE / f'iteration-{args.iteration}'`. Roughly a dozen helpers (grade_completed_runs, prepare_workspace, run_iteration, the report writers) read this global directly instead of taking iteration_dir as a parameter.
findings.rationale: The script already passes `iteration_dir` explicitly to build_performance_report and previous_iteration_dir, so the boundary is half-drawn. Mixing both styles means anyone trying to call run_iteration from a notebook or test will silently get iteration-1 unless they remember to mutate the global first. The `global` rebind in main is the kind of action-at-a-distance that masks bugs when the script grows a second entry point or a unit test.
findings.fix_hint: Compute iteration_dir from args.iteration in main() and thread it through prepare_workspace, run_iteration, grade_completed_runs as an explicit Path. Drop the module-level ITERATION constant or rename it to a default and stop mutating it.

findings.title: grade_one_assertion is a 70-line if/elif registry begging to be a dispatch dict
findings.evidence: Roughly 25 `if name == ...` branches, several with two-name aliases (`{'no-em-dashes-in-rewrite', 'no-em-dashes'}`, `{'no-rewritten-draft', 'no-full-rewrite'}`), each ending with the same shape `return result['passed'], result['evidence']`. The function ends with `raise KeyError(name)`, so it really is a registry lookup pretending to be control flow.
findings.rationale: The pattern bleeds two responsibilities: (a) which assertion name maps to which check, and (b) how to compute the evidence string. Encoding (a) as a dict of callables would let new assertions be added without touching control flow, would make the alias relationships data instead of code, and would let the test surface enumerate supported names. Today this function and `evals.json` drift independently; the dict form would make the registry inspectable.
findings.fix_hint: Define `ASSERTION_HANDLERS: dict[str, Callable[[str, str, str], tuple[bool, str]]]` at module scope, with helper closures for the audit-shape forwards. Aliases become two keys pointing to the same callable. grade_one_assertion becomes a one-liner lookup with a KeyError fallback.

findings.title: Hard-coded /opt/homebrew Python path is environment-specific magic
findings.evidence: `for candidate in ('/opt/homebrew/bin/python3.12', 'python3.12', sys.executable):` — the absolute path only exists on Apple Silicon Homebrew installs. Linux and Intel Macs skip straight to `python3.12` on PATH or fall through to sys.executable.
findings.rationale: Quietly running with the wrong Python (e.g. 3.9) when skill-creator's scripts use match statements or PEP 604 unions is a worse failure than not finding it: the run dies inside an aggregated subprocess with a confusing SyntaxError. Keep the discovery, drop the absolute path, or move it behind a `HUMANISE_PY` env var.
findings.fix_hint: Drop the absolute path; rely on `python3.12` and `python3.13` on PATH plus sys.executable. Surface a clearer error when none satisfies the version check (currently it silently falls back to sys.executable).

findings.title: Codex tempfile leaks on subprocess timeout / FileNotFoundError before read
findings.evidence: `run_codex` creates a tempfile with `delete=False`, then calls `subprocess.run(..., timeout=900)`. The `try/finally` that unlinks the path is wrapped around the *read*, not the subprocess call. If `subprocess.run` raises TimeoutExpired or FileNotFoundError (codex not installed), the tempfile path is leaked and never unlinked.
findings.rationale: These exceptions are caught upstream in run_iteration's threadpool loop, so the workflow continues but `/tmp/humanise-codex-output-*.md` files accumulate across runs. Not a correctness bug, but `delete=False` always wants a paired `try/finally` covering everything that can throw between create and unlink.
findings.fix_hint: Move the tempfile creation inside a single try/finally that also wraps the subprocess.run call, or use a context manager helper that always unlinks on exit regardless of which step raised.

findings.title: Module-loaded grade.py at import time is a startup-cost trap and a test-isolation problem
findings.evidence: `GRADE = load_grade_module()` runs at module import time, executing all of humanise/grade.py (2700+ lines, dozens of compiled regexes) before argparse even sees `--help`. Same pattern in test_grade.py at lines 14-19 (acceptable there).
findings.rationale: For a CLI that takes minutes to run, the millisecond startup penalty is not the issue — the maintenance shape is. The script can't be imported (e.g. by a future test) without the side effect of compiling the whole grader. If grade.py ever grows a `print` at module scope or a missing-dependency import, this script's `--help` breaks. Pythonic alternative is a lazy `_grade()` accessor.
findings.fix_hint: Wrap the module load in `@functools.lru_cache` on a `grade_module()` accessor and rewrite usages from `GRADE.X` to `grade_module().X`. Worth the small refactor because the assertion grader and the body-stats functions are the only callers and they all run after argparse.

findings.title: Broad `except Exception` at the assertion level loses traceback context
findings.evidence: Two places swallow bare `Exception`: in grade_run (`except Exception as exc: passed, evidence = False, f'grader error for {assertion["name"]}: {exc}'`) and in run_iteration's threadpool loop (`except Exception as exc:` writing 'ERROR: {exc}' to response.md). The first comment explicitly says 'keep the viewer useful even when an assertion needs work'.
findings.rationale: I understand the design intent — a viewer that survives one broken grader is better than a hard crash mid-run. But the current shape stringifies the exception only, dropping the traceback. When an assertion grader silently returns False because of an AttributeError three frames deep, the eval results look like a real failure rather than a grader bug. Worth at least logging the traceback to stderr or a sidecar file so the cause is recoverable.
findings.fix_hint: Use `logging.exception(...)` or capture `traceback.format_exc()` into the evidence string / a sibling errors.log so a future debugger can see where it broke without re-running the eval.

findings.title: Detached subprocess.Popen viewer can outlive the parent without being tracked
findings.evidence: Non-static viewer mode does `subprocess.Popen([py, viewer, ...], stdout=log, stderr=subprocess.STDOUT)` and prints `viewer launched; log={log_path}`. Nothing terminates this process when the script exits, and the returned Popen object is discarded.
findings.rationale: Behaviourally fine for an interactive `--no-static-viewer` workflow where Mae kills it manually, but if this script is ever invoked by CI or a wrapper that ends quickly, you get an orphaned long-running Python process bound to a port (the viewer is presumably a server). At minimum, the launch should record the PID into the iteration directory so a later `--stop-viewer` action could clean it up. Today there is no leash.
findings.fix_hint: Write `viewer.pid` next to viewer.log; consider documenting the lifecycle expectation in the docstring. If the viewer is a server, accept that as the deliberate UX, but say so out loud in the print.

findings.title: build_performance_report is a 280-line do-everything that mixes data assembly and Markdown rendering
findings.evidence: Single function gathers per-eval pass rates, corpus rows (with two source paths), group means, pairwise gaps, audit fidelity rows, regression rows; defines three nested helpers (_mean, _group_means, _gap_pct); appends to a `lines` list across roughly 100 lines of f-string Markdown; finally returns both rendered text and a structured dict. Returns a tuple of (markdown, structured) where the structured dict is partially recomputed inline at the bottom.
findings.rationale: Future me cannot test the regression-detection logic (`delta < -0.05`) without going through Markdown rendering. The body-level statistics block is a separate concern from the audit-fidelity block but lives in the same function. The nested closures over `corpus_rows` / `group_summary` make the data dependencies hard to follow — you have to mentally execute 200 lines to know which dict has what keys at which point. This is exactly the readability bar Kieran flags.
findings.fix_hint: Split into pure data-gathering functions (`compute_per_eval`, `compute_corpus_rows`, `compute_group_means`, `compute_audit_fidelity`, `compute_regression_rows`) returning typed dicts/dataclasses, plus a separate `render_performance_markdown(structured) -> str`. The structured dict becomes the single source of truth and the renderer is testable without mocking the filesystem.

findings.title: TypedDicts / dataclasses absent for the heavy dict-shape contracts crossing function boundaries
findings.evidence: Functions return `dict` for richly-shaped payloads — `analyze_sample_body` returns 10 specific keys, `grade_sample_file` returns 5 keys, build_performance_report's structured dict has 7 nested keys. Callers pull `r['body_stats']['sentence_len_mean']` and `corpus_data['groups']` with no static guarantee these keys exist.
findings.rationale: Not lethal — the shapes are local and the dicts are internally consistent — but this is a >1000-LOC harness where every consumer has to remember which keys are present after which optional branch. A handful of TypedDicts (or dataclasses if you prefer constructors) would make the data flow legible without runtime cost. The audit-fidelity row vs corpus-row distinction in particular keeps tripping the reader because both have similar but non-identical fields.
findings.fix_hint: Introduce TypedDict for BodyStats, CorpusRow, FidelityRow, RegressionRow, PerEvalRow, StructuredReport. mypy not required to benefit — the documentation of which keys a function returns is the win.

findings.title: Token-counting branch in run_claude is fragile to schema drift
findings.evidence: Inside `try: parsed = json.loads(raw)` block, the code unconditionally treats parsed list/dict, calls `.get('result') or .get('message') or raw`, then accesses `usage` which may be a list or dict, summing four token fields. The single broad `except json.JSONDecodeError: pass` covers the parse, but downstream `int(...)` calls inside the comprehension can raise TypeError if `entry.get('input_tokens', 0)` returns a non-numeric (e.g. a string the API now returns).
findings.rationale: Claude CLI's JSON output schema is not yours to control. The current shape will silently raise TypeError if the schema changes — and that TypeError is *not* caught by the JSONDecodeError handler. The harness will then fail mid-run inside the threadpool and recovery becomes ugly. Worth narrowing or widening the except to cover (TypeError, ValueError, KeyError) since this is exploratory parsing of an external CLI response.
findings.fix_hint: Either widen to `except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError, ValueError, KeyError): total_tokens = 0` (record the failure to stderr), or extract a `_extract_total_tokens(parsed) -> int` helper with explicit isinstance guards on every numeric access.

findings.title: test_grade.py imports humanise/grade.py via importlib boilerplate every test run
findings.evidence: Six lines of importlib spec/loader plumbing followed by 10 manual rebindings (ALL_CHECKS = _grade.ALL_CHECKS, ...). The previous `sys.path.insert + from grade import` was a one-liner. The change was forced because grade.py now lives under `humanise/` instead of next to test_grade.py.
findings.rationale: The path-twiddling import (`sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2] / 'humanise'))`) followed by `from grade import (...)` would work just as well and would not need the per-symbol unpacking. The current shape reads as 'I had to do this by hand because I couldn't make the package layout work', which is true but worth not paying for in 10+ rebindings. Also: the loader-None check on line 17 is after the call to `module_from_spec(_spec)` on line 16, which would already crash if _spec were None.
findings.fix_hint: Either insert humanise/ onto sys.path and use a normal `from grade import ...` block, or wrap the importlib dance into a single `_load_grade()` helper called once. Move the `if _spec is None or _spec.loader is None` guard before module_from_spec.

findings.title: humanise/grade.py is the canonical module; dev/evals/grade.py does not exist
findings.evidence: The PR description references 'two grade.py files' (humanise/grade.py and dev/evals/grade.py). Filesystem confirms only humanise/grade.py exists in the diff (4056-5050) and on disk; the historical dev/evals/grade.py was removed and now test_grade.py loads from humanise/grade.py via importlib. A separate file at dev/skill-workspace/skill-snapshot/grade.py exists as the pre-rewrite snapshot, used as the old_skill baseline.
findings.rationale: Worth saying out loud because the PR description framed this as a divergence question. There is no live duplication — humanise/grade.py is the only grader. The snapshot is intentional reference material and not on the import path.
findings.fix_hint: No code change needed. If the PR description claimed two files, fix the description. Confirm the snapshot isn't accidentally being imported by any test path.

findings.title: split_sentences and other GRADE-internal helpers are reached through attribute access
findings.evidence: The adapter reaches into GRADE for `GRADE.split_sentences`, `GRADE.CHECK_REPORT_TEXT`, `GRADE.check_audit_shape`, `GRADE.regrade`, `GRADE.annotate_result`, `GRADE.check_manufactured_insight`, `GRADE.check_signposted_conclusions`, `GRADE.grade_file`. None of these are declared in an `__all__` and grade.py prefixes none with `_`, so the boundary is implicit.
findings.rationale: humanise/grade.py is now the public surface for two callers (the CLI and this adapter). Several of the symbols the adapter pulls (split_sentences, check_manufactured_insight) read like internal helpers. As grade.py evolves, anything renamed there silently breaks the adapter. The standard Python answer is `__all__` or at least a small re-exporting facade module that makes the contract explicit.
findings.fix_hint: Add an `__all__` to humanise/grade.py listing the symbols intended for external use, or extract a thin `humanise/grading_api.py` that re-exports the supported surface. Either way, the adapter's reads become a checked contract instead of attribute pokes.

findings.title: ThreadPoolExecutor + 8-way Claude/Codex subprocesses: signal handling and child-process orphaning under Ctrl+C
findings.evidence: `with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=workers) as executor:` running 8 concurrent `subprocess.run(..., timeout=900)` calls of `claude` / `codex`. ThreadPoolExecutor.__exit__ calls shutdown(wait=True), which blocks on all running futures. SIGINT during a run will not stop the child Claude/Codex processes — they hold their own signal handlers and the threadpool will sit waiting on subprocess.run.
findings.rationale: On a 15-minute timeout × 8 workers, an aborted run can leave the user staring at an unresponsive process for up to 15 minutes per stuck worker, with N stale Claude API sessions burning tokens until they time out. Real-world maintenance pain: Mae will Ctrl+C, get nothing, kill -9, and then the tempfiles and viewer.log are not cleaned up. Worth at minimum installing a SIGINT handler that propagates terminate to the child processes, or using `concurrent.futures.ProcessPoolExecutor` with `cancel_futures=True` semantics if applicable. This is the kind of regression risk the parallel rewrite quietly introduced.
findings.fix_hint: Wrap subprocess.run in a helper that registers the Popen child with a global registry; on SIGINT, terminate all live children before re-raising. Or use `subprocess.Popen` + manual wait loop so you can call `.terminate()` from the signal handler.

findings.title: Magic numerical thresholds embedded in assertion logic without named constants
findings.evidence: `return len(hits) >= 5`, `<= 3`, `<= 12`, `int(input_words * 0.45)`, `value > 35`, `213 <= count <= 288`, `delta < -0.05`. Numbers with semantic meaning ('5 catalogue patterns', 'paragraph stdev > 35 words', '5% regression bound') are scattered across grade_one_assertion and build_performance_report.
findings.rationale: These are the calibration knobs of the eval. A reader trying to understand 'why is 213 the lower word-count target' has to grep for the test case in evals.json and infer the connection. Named constants (`MIN_FLAGS_FOR_AI_PROSE = 5`, `REGRESSION_THRESHOLD = -0.05`) make the harness self-documenting and let future tuning happen in one place. Particularly worth it for the regression bound and the audit-fidelity thresholds, which are policy decisions the user (Mae) will tune.
findings.fix_hint: Introduce a small CONSTANTS section near the top of the module with named thresholds and the comment explaining each. Pull paragraph-uniformity stdev > 35 from grade.py if it already lives there, otherwise alias.

residual_risks: The harness is the seam between Mae's hand-tuned eval JSON and skill-creator's upstream tooling; subtle behavioural drift between an iteration's runs and reruns is plausible because non-determinism (Claude, Codex) is silently absorbed into pass-rate means.

test_grade.py runs at module import as a 1300-LOC linear script with mutating global FAILURES counter and direct prints. This is fine as a smoke test but makes it hard to integrate into pytest later. Out of scope for this PR but worth flagging.
testing_gaps: build_performance_report has no unit tests despite encoding the regression-detection policy and the corpus-baseline computation. Splitting it into pure functions (finding kieran-python-8) would make unit-testing the regression rule trivial.

grade_one_assertion (the main dispatch function) is tested only end-to-end via real eval runs. A small parametric test that covers each named assertion with a fixture output would catch the alias drift (`no-em-dashes-in-rewrite` vs `no-em-dashes`) the next time someone renames a check.
No test exercises the SIGINT / threadpool teardown path; the orphan-subprocess concern (kieran-python-14) cannot be regression-protected today.

No test exercises `update_readme_performance_block` — silent no-op when markers are absent is intentional, but the substitution regex on line 930 has no coverage and the README text it produces is untested.
